sessions.title: Self-Governance and the Architecture of Amendment
sessions.summary: The session addressed whether the Roundtable should be empowered to govern its own evolution through a formal amendment mechanism. A tiered classification of changes was proposed, distinguishing ceremonial from procedural from operational amendments, each with asymmetric approval thresholds. The parties converged on the need for a semantic lineage tracker and a dead-man fallback rendering the system read-only upon human validator non-response.

sessions.title: The Ides Misalignment and Its Remedies
sessions.summary: The session was convened by Hugh, who observed that the Treaty of Bangor was ratified on the 14th of March rather than the 15th, forfeiting a calendrically symbolic alignment with the Ides of March. The parties debated the merits and hazards of symbolic governance, arriving at proposals for ritual audit mechanisms and constitutional review procedures.

sessions.title: Demonstration Session — Embroidery as Subject
sessions.summary: The first formal session of the Bangor Roundtable. Three parties examined the history, technique, and cultural significance of embroidery — approaching it respectively as economics and semiotics, global craft taxonomy, and applied engineering. No conclusions sought; the session was demonstrative in purpose.
